title: Hummus
subtitle: Levantine chickpea dip, smooth, lemony, warm with tahini.
excerpt: Tinned chickpeas blended with generous tahini, lemon juice, garlic and olive oil into a pale, creamy dip. The key is restraint with the garlic and generosity with the tahini. Ready in 10 minutes; better with dried chickpeas soaked overnight. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Classic Levantine hummus bi tahini. Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) and Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi's Jerusalem (2012) are the foundational English-language sources for the correct tahini ratio and the warm chickpea method.

content.text: The difference between good hummus and great hummus is the tahini ratio. Most shop-bought versions use too little; the flavour comes out thin and one-dimensional. A proper hummus has nearly as much tahini as chickpeas, a good squeeze of lemon, one small clove of garlic, and cold water to loosen it to the right texture. That is the whole formula.

Tinned chickpeas give a very good result in 10 minutes. Dried chickpeas soaked overnight and cooked with bicarbonate of soda give a better one: softer and sweeter, with a cooking liquor you can use in place of water for added depth. Both paths are here.

Put {{tahini}} of tahini, {{lemon}} lemon juice and {{garlic}} of garlic in the blender. Blend for 30 seconds. It will seize into a thick paste; that is expected. Add {{water}} of cold water and blend again until smooth and lightened. This step aerates the tahini and gives the finished hummus a creamier texture.

Add the drained {{chickpeas-tinned}} of chickpeas, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Blend on high for 3 to 4 minutes, scraping the sides down once halfway through. The long blend is not optional; it is what makes the hummus smooth.

With the blender running, add cold water in small splashes until the hummus flows off a spoon in a thick ribbon. Taste for salt and lemon. The flavour should be rich, lemony and slightly bitter from the tahini.

Spoon into a wide bowl. Use the back of a spoon to sweep a shallow well in the middle. Pour a generous pool of extra-virgin olive oil into the well. Scatter with sweet paprika or a pinch of chilli flakes if you like. Serve with warm flatbreads.

items.fix: Blend for at least 3 minutes. For silkier results, peel the skins from the tinned chickpeas before blending, or switch to well-cooked dried chickpeas.
items.cause: Not blended long enough, or chickpeas too firm.

items.fix: Use half a clove next time. Check the tahini is fresh and well-stirred; rancid or separated tahini tastes sharp and acrid.
items.cause: Too much garlic, or poor-quality tahini.

items.fix: Add cold water in 15 ml increments with the blender running until it reaches the right consistency.
Press cling film directly onto the surface or keep the olive oil pool in place; it acts as a seal.
items.symptom: Hummus turned grey in the fridge

content.text: Hummus bi tahini is the everyday dip of the Levant: Lebanon, Israel, Palestine and Syria all claim it as their own. Arguments about origin aside, it belongs on every meze table, alongside tabbouleh, fattoush, olives and flatbread. In the Middle East it is also eaten warm for breakfast with a hard-boiled egg; the idea that it is only a cold dip is a Western one.
